docsJson.title: MS AI School 8기 스터디 Daily Report

textRun.content: | 오전 1) 인적성  — 완료 2) 서치펌서류제출  — 완료 3) 빅분기 / AICE 신청 —- 내일로 이동  카페 11시 ~ 1시반  오후 1) 면접  — 완료 2) 빅분기/AICE —-- 내일로 이동 3) 원서 10분 —--- 완료 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항  오전 9시 - 10시 : 빅분기 / AICE 신청  부재중 : 오전 10시 - 11시 운동 / 11시 - 2시 카페  오후 2시 1) 빅분기 기출문제  2) 원서 10분 3) 라떼아트연습 | |
| 1. 포트폴리오 정리 사이트까지만 만듦. 3차 프로젝트 정리는 아직   2. 프로그래머스 코딩테스트 lv1 10문제   3. 사이드 프로젝트 시작 구축 중 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 :1. 포트폴리오/프로젝트 정리 계속 진행   2.공고 지원 작성   3.코딩테스트   4.사이드 프로젝트 구축 | |
| 오전) GitHub readme 정리   오후) 자기소개서 초안 작성   저녁) 면접준비 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : Coex AX 박람회 참석 | |

| 1. 자소서 작성 - 오전 중 2시간 정도 - 내용은 완료, 내일 이쁘게 모양 다듬기 -> 오늘 내용 작성은 완료, 다음에 디자인 뽑기 2. 회사 1곳 이상 서치 - 오전 중 1시간 정도 - 10군대 후보군 뽑아봄. 내일 자세히 살피기. -> 후보군 10개 딥리서치 함. 3. 프로그래머스 코테 - 1시간 반 정도 (최소 0~1단계 7문 이상 풀기) -> 코테 0단계 15문제 품 4. 빅분기 교재 선정 및 주문 + 시험 접수 -> 증명사진 있어야 되서 접수는 못함 5. 운동 -> 아직 못함 6. 영어 스픽(20분 가량) + 2주간 읽을 논문 하나 찾기 -> 못함 7. 통계학 공부 (0~15p) 하기. -> 못함 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : 코엑스 박람회? | |
| 1. 정보처리기사 실기 준비 - 개념 복습 및 기출 문제 검색 2. 취업 목표 구체화를 위한 목표회사 리스트 추리기 3. 2,3차 프로젝트 자료 깃허브에 업로드 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : 실기 기출 유형 검토, 사람인이 아닌 다른 채용사이트 구인공고 검색 다만 프로젝트 자료 깃허브에 업로드 깜빡함… 내일 최우선으로 업로드 필요 | |
| 3차 프로젝트 정리 후 깃헙 업로드->링크드인 포스팅/티스토리 포스팅/자소서 수정 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : NeRF 논문 읽고 티스토리에 리뷰 | |
| 1. 직무 / 기업 동향 리서치 2. 자소서 작성 3. 적성 검사 영역 확인 | |  |

| 오전 9시 - 10시 : 빅분기 / AICE 신청  부재중 : 오전 10시 - 11시 운동 / 11시 - 2시 카페  오후 2시 ~ 1) 빅분기 / AICE 신청 2) 빅분기 기출문제 3) 원서 10분 4) 라떼아트연습 > tomorrow  —------------------------------------------- 저녁 1)인적성 추가 요청받음 > 제출 2) 원서 10분만 더! | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항  오전 9시 - 11시 1) 빅분기 기출 2025 2) 원서 10분  오전 11시 - 12시 1. 라떼아트 연습  부재중 오후 (카페 & 외부 일정) | |
| 1. 포트폴리오/프로젝트 정리   2.공고 지원 작성 3개   3.빅분기 필기 신청 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 :   1. 포트폴리오 사이트 이미지 추가 및 링크 정리   2.공고 지원 작성   3.코딩테스트   4.사이드 프로젝트 구축 | |
| 오전) GitHub readme 정리   오후) 자기소개서 초안 작성   저녁) 면접준비 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : Coex AX 박람회 참석 | |

| 1. 자소서 작성 - 오전 중 2시간 정도 - 내용은 완료, 내일 이쁘게 모양 다듬기 -> 오늘 내용 작성은 완료, 다음에 디자인 뽑기 2. 회사 1곳 이상 서치 - 오전 중 1시간 정도 - 10군대 후보군 뽑아봄. 내일 자세히 살피기. -> 후보군 10개 딥리서치 함. 3. 프로그래머스 코테 - 1시간 반 정도 (최소 0~1단계 7문 이상 풀기) -> 코테 0단계 15문제 품 4. 빅분기 교재 선정 및 주문 + 시험 접수 -> 증명사진 있어야 되서 접수는 못함 5. 운동 -> 아직 못함 6. 영어 스픽(20분 가량) + 2주간 읽을 논문 하나 찾기 -> 못함 7. 통계학 공부 (0~15p) 하기. -> 못함 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : 코엑스 박람회? | |

| 1. 깃허브 2,3차 프로젝트 업로드 - 리팩터링 후 수집 파이프라인만 업로드 완료 2. 정처기 실기 1회분 기출문제 풀어보기 - 실제 실기 문제는 20문제였음.. 실제로 기출문제 형식으로 풀어보진 못했고 대신 SQL 유형 문제 50문 풀어봄 3. 회사 방향 정하기 (제미니 딥리서치, 구인공고사이트) - 이부분은 시도하지 못함. 내일 해야함 | | 1. 직무 방향 선정 필요. => 내일 최우선 목표 2. 정처기 실기 손코딩쪽 문제 좀더 풀어볼 것 => C,JAVA,PYTHON 위주 3. 남는 시간이 있다면 추후 회사 공고에 넣을떄 필요한 서류들 정리(포트폴리오,자소서,이력서 기본양식) | |
| 깃헙 리드미 수정 및 링크드인 업로드/NeRF 논문 읽고 리뷰/해커톤 신청, 기업 지원 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : | |
| 오전 9시 - 10시 : 빅분기 / AICE 신청  부재중 : 오전 10시 - 11시 운동 / 11시 - 2시 카페  오후 2시 ~ 1) 빅분기 / AICE 신청 2) 빅분기 기출문제 3) 원서 10분 4) 라떼아트연습 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 | |
| 1. 포트폴리오 정리   2.사이드 프로젝트   3.빅분기/aice 책 구매 완료   4.Gen AI Academy APAC Edition | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 :결석 | |
| 오전, 오후) Resume Site 작성 및 Github Page 배포 (https://jaehyeon0420.github.io/resume/)   저녁) 면접준비 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : 기술 동향 및 사용법 조사하여 프로젝트 아이디어 구상, | |

| 8. 포토폴리오 정리 - 오전 중 2차, 1차, 해커톤 나간거 깃허브 정리 9. 회사 1곳 이상 서치 - 오전 중 1시간 정도 - 10군대 후보군 뽑아본거 살피고 첫번째 지원회사 고르기(대충 둘러봄. 내일.. 늦어도 주말까진 최소 1곳 이상 지원하기) 10. 프로그래머스 코테 - 1시간 반 정도 (최소 0~1단계 7문 이상 풀기) 11. 빅분기 오늘 신청 12. 운동 13. 영어 스픽(20분 가량) + 2주간 읽을 논문 or 원서 찾기 14. 통계학 공부 (0~15p) 하기. | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : | |

| 4. 정처기 실기 손코딩쪽 문제 검색 - 2025년도 기출문제집 수집완, 유형별 연습문제 데이터 수집(약 50문제 분량) , NoteBookLM에서 문제풀이겸 개념 복습 중 5. 취업직무 방향 선정 - 아직 내가 가고 싶은 방향은 못정했으나, 프로젝트 개발 경험에 빗대어 데이터엔지니어 직무쪽 요구 자격증과 경험, 기술스택 검색 중 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : 2025년도 기출문제 1,2회분 정독 및 개념 복습 데이터 엔지니어 및 여타 직무방향 검색 | |
| 깃헙 리드미 수정 및 링크드인 업로드/NeRF 논문 읽고 리뷰/해커톤 신청, 기업 지원 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : 개인 프로젝트 스터디 후 설계 |
| 오전 9시반 - 11시 : 면접 준비 & 라떼아트연습  부재중 : 오전 11시 - 4시반 (카페 & 면접)  오후 4시반 ~ 6시 1) 빅분기 기출문제 2) 원서 10분 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 | |
| 1. 포트폴리오/프로젝트 정리   2.공고 지원 작성 3개 3.빅분기 필기 신청 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 :1. 포트폴리오 사이트 이미지 추가 및 링크 정리 2.공고 지원 작성   3.코딩테스트   4.사이드 프로젝트 구축 | |
| 뉴스 읽기, 프로젝트 기술 조사, 취득 자격증 선정 및 구매, 채용공고 스크랩 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : | |

| 15. 자소서 작성 - 오전 중 2시간 정도 - 내용은 완료, 내일 이쁘게 모양 다듬기 -> 오늘 내용 작성은 완료, 다음에 디자인 뽑기 16. 회사 1곳 이상 서치 - 오전 중 1시간 정도 - 10군대 후보군 뽑아봄. 내일 자세히 살피기. -> 후보군 10개 딥리서치 함. 17. 프로그래머스 코테 - 1시간 반 정도 (최소 0~1단계 7문 이상 풀기) -> 코테 0단계 15문제 품 18. 빅분기 교재 선정 및 주문 + 시험 접수 -> 증명사진 있어야 되서 접수는 못함 19. 운동 -> 아직 못함 20. 영어 스픽(20분 가량) + 2주간 읽을 논문 하나 찾기 -> 못함 21. 통계학 공부 (0~15p) 하기. -> 못함 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : 코엑스 박람회? | |
| 6.  2025년도 기출문제 1,2회분 정독 및 개념 복습 7.  데이터 엔지니어 및 여타 직무방향 검색 | | 4. | |
| 관련 취업 공고 서칭 후 필요 역량 분석/3차 프로젝트 정리 후 깃헙 업로드->링크드인 포스팅/티스토리 포스팅/인스타툰 계정, 패션 계정 정리 후 신설(동일하게 업로드)/자소서 수정 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : NeRF 논문 읽고 티스토리에 리뷰 |

| 오전 9시 ~ 10시 : 준비 & 이동 오전 10시 - 11시 : 운동 오전 11시 - 12시 : 라떼아트 오후 12시 - 2시 : 카페알바  오후 2시부터 스터디 복귀~ 1) 원서 10분 2) 빅분기 기출문제 10개만 3) 서류 지원 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 오전 9시 ~ 10시 : 1. 원서 10분 2. 빅분기 기출문제 10개만  오전 10시 - 3시까지 전 회사 방문 외 일정  오후 4시 부터 스터디 복귀 | |
| 1. 더 해커톤 면접/GCP 워크샵 2. 회사 지원 3. 사이드 프로젝트 4. 구글 해커톤 준비 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 :1. 포트폴리오 사이트 이미지 추가 및 링크 정리 2.공고 지원 작성   3.코딩테스트   4.사이드 프로젝트 구축 | |
| ~ 오후 2시) : 국취제 상담 오후 2시 ~) : 주말 강의 복습, Google Gen AI 영상 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : AI 뉴스 읽기 -> 주말 강의 복습 | |

| 22. 회사 서칭한 곳 중 1군데 지원하기, 포폴 약간 손보기. 23. 프로그래머스 코테 - 1시간 반 정도 (최소 0~1단계 7문 이상 풀기) -> 코테 0단계 15문제 품 24. 빅분기 - 공부 시작. 오늘 훑어보고 기출 문제 보기 25. 운동 - 12시 26. 영어 스픽(20분 가량) + 원서 10p 읽기 27. 통계학 공부 (0~15p) 하기. | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : | |

| 8. 프로그래밍 언어(C,JAVA 부분) 기출문제 연습 - 20문 풀이 9. 깃허브 프로젝트 파트 정리 - 3차 프로젝트 README 수정 10. 이력서에 넣을 내용정리(이력서 뼈대 작성) - 아직 시작 못함 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : 2차 프로젝트 파일 자체는 올릴 수 있지만, 어떤식으로 내 파트를 주장할지 아직 고민임. 내일 좀더 고민 필요 코딩 문제 오답률은 줄었지만 아직 모자란 느낌. 포인터의 개념에 대해 다시 공부할 필요가 있음. 이력서 부분은 단순히 깃허브에서 시간을 까먹은 탓에 들어갈 생각을 못했음. 내일 다시 살펴볼 것 | |
| 구글 해커톤관련 아이디어 구상, 버그 전달/더 해커톤 면접/동향세미나/개인프로젝트 진행 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : 개인 프로젝트 진행/해커톤 준비 | |
| 오전 9시 ~ 10시 : 1. 원서 10분 2. 빅분기 기출문제 10개만  오전 10시 - 11시 : 라구 오전 11시 - 12시 : 서류 검토 (CH) 오후 12시 - 3시 : 전 회사 방문 외 일정 오후 4~5시 부터 스터디 복귀: 1. 서류작성  *저녁운동! | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항  9시 운동 수업! | |
| 1. 공고 지원서 작성 2. 구글 해커톤 프로젝트 구현 진행 3. 영어 공부 4. 코딩 테스트 | | 향후(다음날) 진행사항 : | |

google-doc:1XeBPnqg35ECqdzGkNxl0j9IhJzNz.content:
**preidea/ Tech**

**준비 중인 제품(서비스)에 대해 소개해 주세요 \***

\[The Problem I've Observed\]

AI chip teams face a fragmented workflow:

Circuit teams ask, "How much SRAM SNM margin do we need?"

System teams ask, "What throughput and latency will this SNM give us?"

Model teams ask, "Will SRAM errors affect model accuracy?"

Currently, these questions are answered in separate silos.

There's no principled way to connect them:

SNM margin → bit error rate → token error probability → model accuracy

Result: Conservative designs that consume multiple quarters of engineering time and tens of millions of dollars in unnecessary margin.

New pressure: Agentic inference and long-context LLMs are breaking assumptions about SRAM access patterns and failure modes. Teams need a fresh framework.

\[What I've Built\]

I've built partial systems that expose where existing abstractions break:

SRAM circuit-level modeling (Perceptron-based noise prediction)

Reveals that temperature/voltage effects are more complex than linear models assume

Transformer memory profiling (structure-based, not model-specific)

LLaMA, GPT, Gemini all reduce to the same parameters

Shows that generalization is possible across model families

Design space exploration framework

Can enumerate meaningful configurations

But cannot evaluate which are "right" for your system

\[Where I'm Blocked\]

The critical gap is a bridge that doesn't exist:

"How does a single-bit SRAM error translate to actual model accuracy degradation?"

This isn't a simulation problem. It's an abstraction problem:

Bit errors are measured in mV (circuit domain)

Model accuracy is measured in % (model domain)

The relationship depends entirely on your specific LLMs, workloads, and deployment criteria

I can measure one side precisely. I cannot define the other side.

I only encountered this gap after trying to build the bridge myself.

\[A Working Hypothesis I Want to Test\]

One hypothesis I have is that a perceptron-gate–based SRAM simulation can serve as a controllable abstraction layer between circuit noise and model-level behavior.

I chose a perceptron-gate abstraction because it is expressive enough to model non-linear noise interactions while remaining interpretable as a decision boundary over bit stability. This lets me map circuit-level noise (voltage, temperature, margins) directly to bit-state predictions without losing the physical grounding.

In my current setup, the perceptron gate models SRAM bit stability, and its noise weights can be conditioned on test parameters such as voltage, temperature, and timing margins. My hypothesis is that, by further conditioning these noise weights on LLM-specific memory access patterns (e.g., KV-cache usage, attention locality), the simulation can produce error distributions that are more representative of real inference behavior.

This would not "solve" the SNM → accuracy mapping universally. But it could define a family of falsifiable mappings that can be validated—or rejected—using your internal failure data.

Concretely, I want to test this by injecting controlled error distributions into specific transformer subsystems (e.g., attention scores vs. KV-cache) and observing which error patterns correlate with disproportionate accuracy loss. The goal is not to predict accuracy precisely, but to identify which abstractions are stable and which collapse under realistic noise.

If the perceptron abstraction proves insufficient, that failure itself would indicate where more expressive—or entirely different—abstractions are required.

The key question I want to answer is not whether this model is correct, but where it breaks and what that tells us about the right abstraction boundary.

\[Why This Matters\]

If this bridge existed, everything else would become mechanical:

Circuit teams could make decisions in "model reliability" language

System teams could validate against real SRAM constraints

Both teams could converge on design points in days instead of months

But the bridge is the work. Everything else is scaffolding.

\[The Real Question\]

This is not a tooling problem. It's an abstraction problem:

Deciding at what level SRAM unreliability should be absorbed by hardware,

system software, or model behavior.

That's a question that requires your inside knowledge.

I don't think this abstraction can be designed in isolation.

**이전에 어떤 제품(서비스)을 개발해 보신 적이 있나요? \***

\[Professional Background\]

Samsung Electronics (Foundry Division), Hwaseong | 2024

Process Integration Engineer

Worked on BEOL integration for sub-5nm-class logic products:

Participated in cross-functional characterization identifying defect mechanisms

Supported metal stack and process requirement analysis through direct collaboration with customers

Assisted in via-chain yield analysis and process window optimization

Observed: circuit-team margin specifications often didn't align with system-level performance realities

SEMES (Semiconductor Equipment Company) | Jan 2022 – Feb 2024

Junior Etch Process Engineer

Supported plasma etch process development for Far-BEOL interconnect on 300 mm logic devices:

Contributed to etch uniformity optimization and defect reduction efforts

Assisted in troubleshooting chamber stability issues (ESC temperature and gas-flow analysis)

Collaborated with foundry customers on process feasibility and timeline discussions

Key observation: When hardware margins are set conservatively without understanding system context,

Teams spend significant time and resources on unnecessary over-design.

\[AI School: Learning Through Boundaries\]

Project 1: "Multi-Model Vehicle Damage Assessment with Bayesian Confidence Fusion"

Built: MaskRCNN-based damage region segmentation + CustomVision classification integration

My role: Experimented with MaskRCNN for real-camera image data and designed its integration

Technical work: Trained PyTorch models on CUDA via Azure ML Studio

Designed: Bayesian confidence-interval fusion logic connecting segmentation and classification outputs

Result: Created inference pipeline that combines two independent model predictions into unified cost estimates

Learned: Individual models can be accurate in isolation, but their integration requires a bridge layer—and that bridge needs external knowledge (cost-accuracy trade-offs in this case)

Key insight: Two well-tuned models can fail when combined because the integration layer itself is a design problem.

Project 2: "RAG-Based HR SaaS for Seamless Knowledge Transfer (PM Role)"

Problem: Handoff documents consistently fail when real-world context matters (shift workers, departures, new hires, transfers)

Philosophy: "Good handoff documentation should let new staff start work immediately."

My role: Helped define service requirements and knowledge representation strategy

Technical approach: Designed JSON schema capturing essential handoff elements (role, workflows, key contacts, exceptions)

Built: Customizable handoff templates where users can select department/task-specific variations

Enhanced: Converts user-uploaded data → JSON → RAG indexing for searchable knowledge

Infrastructure: Used Azure resources to ensure B2B-grade security

Learned: The right abstraction (JSON schema) determines whether knowledge actually transfers across contexts

Key insight: When knowledge is poorly abstracted, context is lost—no amount of data fixes this. When it's well-abstracted, it scales.

\[The Pattern\]

In Samsung: circuit teams decided margins independently of system teams → conservative-by-default waste.

In AI School: models worked independently but broke when integrated without the right bridge.

Pattern: When subsystems don't connect, the problem isn't one subsystem—it's the abstraction between them.

**OpenAI Grove에서 얻고자 하는 것이 무엇인가요?  \***

\[Why I Can't Solve This Alone\]

My isolation reveals the problem, but isolation can't solve it.

I need to know:

How your specific LLM architectures respond to SRAM errors

What your deployment accuracy requirements actually are

Whether system software can compensate for hardware unreliability

These are empirical questions that require your data and your constraints.

【Why OpenAI】

Three reasons this problem is acute here, not elsewhere:

Your workloads are changing what SRAM needs to be

Agentic inference, long-context, speculative decoding—existing SRAM design assumptions are actively breaking

You have the organizational structure to answer this

Circuit teams, system teams, and model teams exist and can collaborate

Most companies either lack this structure or have it siloed

You care about the answer

SRAM margin directly affects your inference efficiency

This isn't academic. It's strategic.

\[What I Want from Grove\]

Five weeks to move from "isolated systems" to "system understanding":

Week 1 (in SF):

Understand your actual SRAM design process and constraints

Access representative workload traces

Identify what current assumptions are breaking down

Weeks 2-4 (remote):

Produce a matrix mapping SRAM error types to affected model subsystems

Identify 2–3 abstraction strategies that fail consistently across workloads

Document one abstraction that appears stable, and why

Concretely, the output would be a ranked list of which SRAM failure modes are empirically visible to each transformer component (attention, KV-cache, residual paths), paired with the abstraction strategies that either mask or fail to mask these errors. Circuit teams would use this directly to decide which margins actually matter.

Week 5 (in SF):

Present findings: "Here's what was wrong. Here's what's next."

\[Independent Output\]

Even without access to proprietary failure data, I can produce concrete artifacts during Grove:

A taxonomy of SRAM-to-model failure modes

A set of falsifiable hypotheses about which abstractions hold under which workloads

Internal data would validate or refute these hypotheses, but the structure of the questions—and the experimental scaffolding—can be built independently. This means the collaboration has real value even if some internal constraints prevent full data access.

\[Outcome\]

The outcome should enable one concrete decision:

whether SRAM margin should continue to be treated as a fixed hardware requirement,

or as a negotiable parameter co-designed with models and systems.

This would address:

Which assumptions are empirically incorrect?

Where should the abstraction boundary actually be?

Is this solvable as a standalone project, or does it need ongoing partnership?

My role in Grove would be to act as a translator: making failure modes legible across circuit, system, and model boundaries, even when the answer is that no clean translation exists.

I am not proposing to solve this problem in five weeks. The goal of Grove is to identify which abstractions fail under real OpenAI workloads and which ones are worth deeper, long-term collaboration.

After 5 weeks, I want to know where the work actually is.

**더 알려주고 싶으신 내용이 있나요? \***

\[The Three Questions That Drive This\]

At what level of abstraction does SRAM unreliability stop being a circuit problem and start being a system or model problem?

This determines everything about architecture, tools, and responsibility boundaries.

Can model-level techniques (aggressive decoding, quantization, architecture changes) absorb SRAM variability, or does hardware always have to be conservative?

If yes, we've been over-designing circuits.

If not, we need a different approach entirely.

Are speculative decoding and agentic inference fundamentally changing the margins, or just shifting when they occur?

This determines whether existing design philosophy still works.

\[Why Grove\]

This problem sits at a boundary nobody's really solved:

circuit physics & system architecture & model behavior

Agentic and long-context workloads make this decision unavoidable.

If this boundary question is already becoming unavoidable inside OpenAI,

Grove seems like the right place to confront it rigorously.

textRun.content: 준비 중인 제품(서비스)에 대해 소개해 주세요 *
AI chip teams face a fragmented workflow:
Circuit teams ask, "How much SRAM SNM margin do we need?"
System teams ask, "What throughput and latency will this SNM give us?"
Model teams ask, "Will SRAM errors affect model accuracy?"
Currently, these questions are answered in separate silos.
There's no principled way to connect them:
SNM margin → bit error rate → token error probability → model accuracy
Result: Conservative designs that consume multiple quarters of engineering time and tens of millions of dollars in unnecessary margin.
New pressure: Agentic inference and long-context LLMs are breaking assumptions about SRAM access patterns and failure modes. Teams need a fresh framework.
I've built partial systems that expose where existing abstractions break:
Reveals that temperature/voltage effects are more complex than linear models assume
LLaMA, GPT, Gemini all reduce to the same parameters
Shows that generalization is possible across model families
But cannot evaluate which are "right" for your system
The critical gap is a bridge that doesn't exist:
"How does a single-bit SRAM error translate to actual model accuracy degradation?"
This isn't a simulation problem. It's an abstraction problem:
Bit errors are measured in mV (circuit domain)
Model accuracy is measured in % (model domain)
The relationship depends entirely on your specific LLMs, workloads, and deployment criteria
I can measure one side precisely. I cannot define the other side.
I only encountered this gap after trying to build the bridge myself.
[A Working Hypothesis I Want to Test]
One hypothesis I have is that a perceptron-gate–based SRAM simulation can serve as a controllable abstraction layer between circuit noise and model-level behavior.

I chose a perceptron-gate abstraction because it is expressive enough to model non-linear noise interactions while remaining interpretable as a decision boundary over bit stability. This lets me map circuit-level noise (voltage, temperature, margins) directly to bit-state predictions without losing the physical grounding.

In my current setup, the perceptron gate models SRAM bit stability, and its noise weights can be conditioned on test parameters such as voltage, temperature, and timing margins. My hypothesis is that, by further conditioning these noise weights on LLM-specific memory access patterns (e.g., KV-cache usage, attention locality), the simulation can produce error distributions that are more representative of real inference behavior.
This would not "solve" the SNM → accuracy mapping universally. But it could define a family of falsifiable mappings that can be validated—or rejected—using your internal failure data.

Concretely, I want to test this by injecting controlled error distributions into specific transformer subsystems (e.g., attention scores vs. KV-cache) and observing which error patterns correlate with disproportionate accuracy loss. The goal is not to predict accuracy precisely, but to identify which abstractions are stable and which collapse under realistic noise.
If the perceptron abstraction proves insufficient, that failure itself would indicate where more expressive—or entirely different—abstractions are required.
The key question I want to answer is not whether this model is correct, but where it breaks and what that tells us about the right abstraction boundary.
If this bridge existed, everything else would become mechanical:
Circuit teams could make decisions in "model reliability" language
System teams could validate against real SRAM constraints
Both teams could converge on design points in days instead of months
But the bridge is the work. Everything else is scaffolding.
This is not a tooling problem. It's an abstraction problem:
Deciding at what level SRAM unreliability should be absorbed by hardware,
That's a question that requires your inside knowledge.
I don't think this abstraction can be designed in isolation.
이전에 어떤 제품(서비스)을 개발해 보신 적이 있나요? *
Worked on BEOL integration for sub-5nm-class logic products:
Participated in cross-functional characterization identifying defect mechanisms
Supported metal stack and process requirement analysis through direct collaboration with customers
Assisted in via-chain yield analysis and process window optimization
Observed: circuit-team margin specifications often didn't align with system-level performance realities
Supported plasma etch process development for Far-BEOL interconnect on 300 mm logic devices:
Contributed to etch uniformity optimization and defect reduction efforts
Assisted in troubleshooting chamber stability issues (ESC temperature and gas-flow analysis)
Collaborated with foundry customers on process feasibility and timeline discussions
Key observation: When hardware margins are set conservatively without understanding system context,
Teams spend significant time and resources on unnecessary over-design.
Project 1: "Multi-Model Vehicle Damage Assessment with Bayesian Confidence Fusion"
Built: MaskRCNN-based damage region segmentation + CustomVision classification integration
My role: Experimented with MaskRCNN for real-camera image data and designed its integration
Technical work: Trained PyTorch models on CUDA via Azure ML Studio
Designed: Bayesian confidence-interval fusion logic connecting segmentation and classification outputs
Result: Created inference pipeline that combines two independent model predictions into unified cost estimates
Learned: Individual models can be accurate in isolation, but their integration requires a bridge layer—and that bridge needs external knowledge (cost-accuracy trade-offs in this case)
Key insight: Two well-tuned models can fail when combined because the integration layer itself is a design problem.
Project 2: "RAG-Based HR SaaS for Seamless Knowledge Transfer (PM Role)"
Problem: Handoff documents consistently fail when real-world context matters (shift workers, departures, new hires, transfers)
Philosophy: "Good handoff documentation should let new staff start work immediately."
My role: Helped define service requirements and knowledge representation strategy
Technical approach: Designed JSON schema capturing essential handoff elements (role, workflows, key contacts, exceptions)
Built: Customizable handoff templates where users can select department/task-specific variations
Enhanced: Converts user-uploaded data → JSON → RAG indexing for searchable knowledge
Infrastructure: Used Azure resources to ensure B2B-grade security
Learned: The right abstraction (JSON schema) determines whether knowledge actually transfers across contexts
Key insight: When knowledge is poorly abstracted, context is lost—no amount of data fixes this. When it's well-abstracted, it scales.
In Samsung: circuit teams decided margins independently of system teams → conservative-by-default waste.
In AI School: models worked independently but broke when integrated without the right bridge.
Pattern: When subsystems don't connect, the problem isn't one subsystem—it's the abstraction between them.
OpenAI Grove에서 얻고자 하는 것이 무엇인가요?  *
My isolation reveals the problem, but isolation can't solve it.
How your specific LLM architectures respond to SRAM errors
What your deployment accuracy requirements actually are
Whether system software can compensate for hardware unreliability
These are empirical questions that require your data and your constraints.
Three reasons this problem is acute here, not elsewhere:
Your workloads are changing what SRAM needs to be
Agentic inference, long-context, speculative decoding—existing SRAM design assumptions are actively breaking
You have the organizational structure to answer this
Circuit teams, system teams, and model teams exist and can collaborate
Most companies either lack this structure or have it siloed
SRAM margin directly affects your inference efficiency
Five weeks to move from "isolated systems" to "system understanding":
Understand your actual SRAM design process and constraints
Identify what current assumptions are breaking down
Produce a matrix mapping SRAM error types to affected model subsystems
Identify 2–3 abstraction strategies that fail consistently across workloads
Document one abstraction that appears stable, and why

Concretely, the output would be a ranked list of which SRAM failure modes are empirically visible to each transformer component (attention, KV-cache, residual paths), paired with the abstraction strategies that either mask or fail to mask these errors. Circuit teams would use this directly to decide which margins actually matter.
Present findings: "Here's what was wrong. Here's what's next."

A taxonomy of SRAM-to-model failure modes
A set of falsifiable hypotheses about which abstractions hold under which workloads

Internal data would validate or refute these hypotheses, but the structure of the questions—and the experimental scaffolding—can be built independently. This means the collaboration has real value even if some internal constraints prevent full data access.
The outcome should enable one concrete decision:
whether SRAM margin should continue to be treated as a fixed hardware requirement,
or as a negotiable parameter co-designed with models and systems.
Where should the abstraction boundary actually be?
Is this solvable as a standalone project, or does it need ongoing partnership?
My role in Grove would be to act as a translator: making failure modes legible across circuit, system, and model boundaries, even when the answer is that no clean translation exists.
I am not proposing to solve this problem in five weeks. The goal of Grove is to identify which abstractions fail under real OpenAI workloads and which ones are worth deeper, long-term collaboration.
After 5 weeks, I want to know where the work actually is.

Can model-level techniques (aggressive decoding, quantization, architecture changes) absorb SRAM variability, or does hardware always have to be conservative?
